artifacts.content:
Hi Mags,

I just made myself a half-caf and figured I’d sit down and actually map out the next few months before everything with Dad, Dan, and my knee starts colliding.

Right now I’m telling people I can realistically commit to things through about December 2026, but with Dad’s Parkinson’s creeping along and Dr. Patel at Mayo Clinic–Eau Claire talking on October 3, 2025 about “probably needing to look at Dove Healthcare within 12–18 months,” I’m trying to hold it all loosely. If Dad has to move from the condo into Dove and I’m in Eau Claire several days a week, I’ll have to scale back on everything here.

On top of that, I’ve still got that tentative knee replacement with Dr. Larson at Methodist in St. Louis Park circled for February 2026. If we go ahead, I’ll be out of commission for a bit and probably doing my own rehab at North Lakes Outpatient Rehab, which is a strange role reversal. I keep picturing myself on the other side of the therapy mat.

And then there’s Dan. After the partial hospitalizations at Abbott Northwestern in May 2024 and again this past January 2025, his depression feels like this big question mark hanging over any long-term plan. If he ends up needing long‑term disability from Southwest High, I may need to be around more for him too.

All of that is why I’m so focused on calendars and “12‑month commitments” right now. I don’t want to overpromise and then vanish on people. Some days it feels like my life is just color‑coding who might fall apart when.

Anyway, enough doom and gloom. How are Ryan and the kids? Send me their recital dates so I can try to come over for at least one thing this spring.

Love,
Evelyn

metadata.subject: Trying to see the road ahead a little

artifacts.content:
Hi Laura,

I’ve been thinking about how this next season is going to look, and I wanted you to hear it from me instead of in bits and pieces over rushed Sunday calls.

I’ve decided I’m going to give myself about a 12‑month window, starting around March 2026, where I say “yes” to some things that feel meaningful, but I’m also building in escape hatches in case life throws more curveballs. Between your dad’s recurrent major depressive episodes – the partial hospitalizations at Abbott Northwestern in May 2024 and again this January 2025 are still pretty fresh – and Grandpa Tom’s Parkinson’s and memory stuff, I can’t pretend everything is stable.

You know how every other Tuesday evening I drive Grandpa over to Mayo Clinic Health System in Eau Claire for his memory care check‑ins with the neurology team since that mild cognitive impairment diagnosis back in March 2023. Those trips plus the one Sunday a month that I’m in Eau Claire with Aunt Maggie sorting through his condo association fees and those leftover Sacred Heart Hospital bills from his February 2024 hospitalization already make the calendar look like a jigsaw puzzle.

Add in my own knee – Dr. Larson and I are still eyeing February 2026 at Methodist for the replacement – and the fact that I’m paying down that $8,400 IRS mess from the 2022 per‑diem underpayment at North Lakes, and you can probably see why I’m cautious about taking on too much.

All that to say: I’m okay, really. Just trying to be honest about my limits so I can keep showing up for you, Michael, Dad, and Grandpa without burning out.

Love you,
Mom

metadata.subject: How I’m thinking about the next year or so

artifacts.content:
Therapy reflections – Minneapolis Counseling Center – Tuesday 2 p.m. with Dr. Lutz

Things to bring up this week:

1. Grandma Helen guilt loop
- Still thinking about those last six months before she died in March 2018, when she was at Maple Grove Assisted Living in Eau Claire and I felt like I was always driving back to Minneapolis instead of staying one more night.
- Mom and Aunt Carol were so burned out by then; I watched them run themselves ragged.
- Dr. Karen Lutz keeps reminding me that I can’t go back to 2017–2018, but I can choose how I show up for older adults now.

2. COVID telehealth memories
- Spring/Summer 2020: three months of doing North Lakes telehealth out of the spare bedroom here at 4827 Linden Grove Lane.
- Calling Willow Creek Assisted Living in Wisconsin and hearing that nurse say in June 2020 they’d lost five residents in a month and didn’t have enough staff to just sit and talk with people.
- That’s when I promised myself that once I wasn’t working 40+ hours a week, I’d find a senior community where I could be physically present.

3. Work values vs. North Lakes politics
- Written warning from Jenna in May 2023 for “pushing back” on productivity with fragile stroke patients, then the July 2023 hours cut.
- How that pushed me toward wanting settings where dignity and connection matter more than billable units.

4. Connecting the dots
- Volunteering with seniors now feels like a way to honor Grandma Helen (Appleton years, macular degeneration, Go Fish marathons) and to transform some of the helplessness from COVID and Dad’s hospital/rehab stays.
- Ask Dr. Lutz for ideas on boundaries so I don’t over‑identify with every resident.

Event: Drive Dad to Mayo – memory clinic + errands

When: Every other Tuesday, 4:30–9:30 p.m.
Location: Mayo Clinic Health System – Eau Claire, Neurology/Memory Care
Attendees: Dad (Tom Harper), me

Notes:
- Leave Minneapolis by 2:00 p.m. to beat traffic on 94.
- This is the standing memory care check‑in schedule we’ve had since his mild cognitive impairment diagnosis back in March 2023.
- Bring: folder with updated med list, blood pressure log, list of any new falls, and questions about Parkinson’s symptoms.
- Ask neurology team to clarify how MCI + Parkinson’s are interacting and whether we’re still on track with Dr. Patel’s October 3, 2025 timeline about “likely needing to consider Dove Healthcare assisted living within 12–18 months.”
- After appointment: quick stop at his condo to check mail, water plants, and make sure he hasn’t misplaced the Sacred Heart Hospital and Riverfront Transitional Care statements from his February 2022 fall/11‑day stay/three‑week rehab saga.
- If time, swing by Eau Claire Senior Center to pick up their new activities calendar; see if any programs might help with his right‑side weakness from the April 2022 stroke.
- Text Maggie from the parking lot with updates so we’re on the same page for our one‑Sunday‑a‑month bill‑paying visits (condo association fees, lingering medical bills).

Reminder: Do not schedule evening commitments in Minneapolis on these Tuesdays – I’m usually wiped by the time I get home.

metadata.title: Drive Dad to Mayo – memory clinic
metadata.location: Mayo Clinic Health System, Eau Claire, WI

artifacts.content:
Hi Dan,

Just wanted to get tomorrow and the rest of the month straight while I’m actually looking at the calendar.

First, this Sunday is my Eau Claire run – I’ll head out after church, probably around 11, so I can be at Dad’s condo by 1:30. Maggie and I are planning our usual “one‑Sunday‑a‑month” routine: spreading out his Sacred Heart Hospital paperwork from the February 2024 hospitalization, the older Sacred Heart/Riverfront Transitional Care bills from his February 2022 fall, and the stack of overdue condo association fee notices. I’ll bring the checkbook and see what we can negotiate.

Between that, the every‑other‑Tuesday memory clinic runs to Mayo Clinic Health System in Eau Claire for his MCI check‑ins, and my second‑Monday‑of‑the‑month financial counseling at Lutheran Social Service downtown about the $18,000 we still owe from your 2021 heart procedure at Abbott Northwestern, I feel like half my life is hospital parking ramps and billing offices.

Add in the first and third Thursday dementia caregiver group at Jewish Family and Children’s Service from 5–6:30 p.m., and the last‑Thursday‑of‑the‑month grief group here at the house at 7, and I’m realizing why I’m so tired by Friday nights.

Can you handle groceries and a simple dinner on the Eau Claire Sunday? Maybe just throw something in the crockpot before I leave. If Michael comes by, tell him he’s on dishwasher duty.

Also, please put those LSS and JFCS times into your phone so you’re not surprised when I’m dashing out the door.

Love,
Evelyn

metadata.subject: Eau Claire Sunday + all the appointments

artifacts.content:
Personal planning – caregiving & volunteering balance (June 2025)

1. Dad – Eau Claire
- February 2022: fall in condo parking lot → 11 days at Sacred Heart Hospital, then three weeks at Riverfront Transitional Care; $4,300 in out‑of‑pocket bills we haggled over with their billing office.
- February 2024: another hospitalization at Sacred Heart; now monthly Sunday trips with Maggie to sort condo association fees + medical bills.
- March 2023: mild cognitive impairment diagnosis → every‑other‑Tuesday memory clinic at Mayo Clinic Health System in Eau Claire.
- Parkinson’s progressing; Dr. Patel at Mayo–Eau Claire said on 10/3/25 we may need to move him from the condo to Dove Healthcare within 12–18 months.

2. Dan – mental health
- Partial hospitalization at Abbott Northwestern for major depression in May 2024 and again January 2025; watch for signs he’s sliding.
- If he has to go on long‑term disability from Southwest High, I’ll need more flexibility.

3. Me – body & money
- Advanced knee OA – tentative knee replacement with Dr. Larson at Methodist Hospital in Feb 2026; expect 8–12 weeks of rehab at North Lakes.
- Still owe IRS $8,400 from 2022 per‑diem underpayment at North Lakes; wage garnishment in March 2025 was a wake‑up call.

4. What this means
- I can offer roughly 6 hours/week of steady volunteering with seniors through at least August 2026, but I need to be honest that after that, Dad’s move, Dan’s health, surgery, and finances could all force me to cut back.
- Non‑negotiables: Tuesday Mayo runs, LSS 2nd Mondays, JFCS 1st/3rd Thursdays, grief group last Thursdays.
- Keep Sundays after 2 p.m. mostly open except Eau Claire bill‑pay trips.

Hi Karen,

I was thinking about you and the old Tuesday “Gentle Chair Movement & Memory Lane” group at North Lakes, and I wanted to share how all those pieces have been swirling together lately.

Do you remember Fall 2019 through March 2020, when we’d have 6–10 of your MCI/early Alzheimer’s folks in that little group room for 45 minutes of seated exercises and then reminiscence about childhood homes and favorite 40s–60s songs? When COVID hit and we shut the group down, I spent three months doing telehealth from the spare bedroom here at 4827 Linden Grove Lane, and it never felt the same.

Around that time I was also calling Willow Creek Assisted Living in Wisconsin to check on some of Grandma Helen’s old friends. One of the nurses told me in June 2020 they’d lost five residents in a month and just didn’t have enough hands to sit and talk with everyone. That conversation is still lodged in my chest.

Fast‑forward to May 2023: the written warning from Jenna for pushing back on productivity with those frail stroke patients, the budget cuts, and then my hours getting reduced in July. It’s no surprise I’ve been craving spaces where dignity matters more than billable units.

Meanwhile, on the home front, I’ve leaned heavily on my Thursday 1–4 p.m. tech‑help shift at the Linden Hills Senior Center, especially during Daniel’s depression flare in Fall 2021 and then again after his partial hospitalizations at Abbott Northwestern in May 2024 and January 2025. Three hours helping 5–7 older adults set up Zoom calls and email grandkids has been the most grounding part of my week.

All of this is a long way of saying: I’m slowly stitching together a life that feels more aligned with why I went into OT in the first place.

Warmly,
Evelyn
